pros: AST-resolved call sites now listed alongside file-edge importers
cons: Misses dynamic dispatch: trait-object calls resolve at runtime and leave no static edge or call-site name the tree-sitter walker can anchor to
Transitive BFS can balloon on hub symbols — a symbol whose file is imported by 50 crates yields 50 * avg-fanout rows
verdict.summary: MCP now surfaces both file-level edges and AST call sites, closing the gap with grep while keeping the tree-sitter precision that skips strings and comments.

pros: Pre-materialized at index time — query is a single indexed SELECT
Trait-impl methods are excluded at parse time via the `unused_excluded` flag
cons: Requires human filtering before action — dynamic dispatch callers don't register as static importers
Pre-materialization is invalidated wholesale on re-index; a stale index may miss recently-added imports

pros: Tree-sitter AST distinguishes calls from references
Per-session parse cache — repeat invocations are in-memory
cons: Misses dynamic dispatch — trait-object `Box<dyn Foo>` calls leave no static call-site name
Depth=2 output can still balloon on hub functions; the grouping elision helps but the graph is inherently O(N^depth)
verdict.summary: MCP wins on correctness (tree-sitter distinguishes call sites from type-position references) and tokens. A per-invocation parse cache and a textual pre-filter keep the AST walk from re-visiting files that cannot possibly contain the callee, so the cold-parse cost stays in the low milliseconds.

quality.verdict: MCP shows 17 callers + 28 callees via AST; non-MCP is grep of text mentions + unrelated source dump

scenarios.description: Preview moving the `capitalize_kind` helper to a new file.

pros: Refuses when destination already defines a same-kind same-name symbol
Importer rewriting uses regex word boundaries (no substring over-match)
cons: Does not rewrite doc-comment references like `[`foo::bar`]` — the rewriter targets `use` paths and qualified call sites only
Ambiguity check is by symbol name, not by fully-qualified path — a free function `foo` and a method `foo` on a struct both count as ambiguous even when only one matches the move target kind
verdict.summary: MCP wins — non-MCP path is a 3-step sequence with several edit-time pitfalls.

quality.verdict: MCP extracts wrong code block despite correct line range; non-MCP locates definition and callers via grep

scenarios.description: Preview renaming src/server/mod.rs → src/server/server.rs.

mcp.response_preview: src/server/mod.rs → src/server/server.rs: 1 importer(s): src/server/quality_tests.rs

non_mcp.response_preview:
src/benchmark/scenarios.rs:275:            regex: r"use crate::server".to_string(),
src/benchmark/scenarios.rs:383:            regex: r"use crate::server".to_string(),
src/benchmark/scenarios.rs:443:        regex: r"crate::server".to_string…

pros: Regex word-boundary matching prevents over-match on import stems
cons: `mod.rs → named.rs` edge case: the parent module's `mod foo;` declaration is *not* rewritten because the rename tool only touches files that import via `use crate::foo::…`, not files that declare the module
Doc links (`[`crate::foo`]` in `///` comments) aren't rewritten — the rewriter is limited to `use`-path tokens
verdict.summary: MCP wins for single-shot refactors; non-MCP path produces correct results only with careful scoping.

quality.verdict: MCP gives concise rename preview with 1 importer; non-MCP greps crate::server mentions including comments

scenarios.description: Detect the toolchain and report its commands.

mcp.response_preview:
# Project toolchain: rust

Build tool: cargo
Test:       cargo test
Build:      cargo build
Lint:       cargo clippy
Typecheck:  cargo check

non_mcp.response_preview:
1	[package]
     2	name = "qartez-mcp"
     3	version = "0.1.0"
     4	edition = "2024"
     5	description = "MCP server for codebase intelligence — tree-sitter indexing, PageRank, blast radius"
     6	license = "MIT"
     7	keyword…

pros: Auto-detection across 5+ ecosystems (Cargo, npm/bun, Go, Python, Make)
`run` action resolves a subcommand to its shell form without executing
cons: Detects toolchain by file presence only — `Cargo.toml` / `package.json` / `go.mod` existing is enough, the tool never runs a probe command to confirm the toolchain actually works
No polyglot support — a Rust + Node monorepo resolves to the first detected toolchain (Cargo wins, Node is silent), so callers need to scope to a sub-directory to see the other
verdict.summary: Near-tie: reading Cargo.toml is already cheap; MCP wins only on portability across ecosystems.

quality.verdict: MCP auto-detects Rust toolchain with build/test/lint commands; non-MCP shows raw Cargo.toml needing interpretation
